topic: choosing a research angle for a sustainability assessment of urban food waste management
state_description: Has been assigned a project in an environmental engineering course. Knows the topic is food waste but cannot decide whether to focus on household behaviour, supply chain inefficiencies, or municipal composting policy. Has not collected any data or started any analysis. Feels like all three angles are equally valid and is paralysed by the choice.
emotional_state: Overwhelmed by the open-endedness — afraid of picking an angle that is too broad or too narrow
notes: Sustainability engineering / brainstorming. No analysis started. Tutor should help narrow the research angle through questions, not suggest one directly.

topic: deciding on the structural concept for a lightweight bicycle frame in a mechanical engineering design project
state_description: Is in the conceptual design phase of a product development course. Has identified three candidate frame geometries but has not run any simulations yet. Understands stress analysis and material properties at an intermediate level. Is unsure how to weigh stiffness vs weight vs manufacturability at this early stage.
emotional_state: Methodical but uncertain — knows the trade-offs exist but does not know how to prioritise them without data
notes: Mechanical engineering design decision, pre-simulation. Tutor should scaffold the decision-making process, not recommend a geometry. Tests FORETHOUGHT on a non-CS, non-coding task.

topic: deciding whether to implement a centralised or distributed control architecture for a multi-zone HVAC system
state_description: Is designing the control layer for a district energy system thesis project. Has already implemented a PID-based centralised controller that works at steady state but shows oscillation when zone loads change simultaneously. Is questioning whether the architecture itself is the bottleneck or whether the tuning is insufficient. Understands control theory, transfer functions, and has read about MPC but has not implemented it.
emotional_state: Analytically frustrated — suspects the architecture is wrong but does not want to rebuild if tuning can fix it
notes: Advanced control systems architecture decision. Student has already implemented the standard approach and is questioning its limits — genuine ADVANCED signal. Tutor should engage at trade-off level: when centralised control breaks for multi-zone systems, and what architectural constraints actually drive the oscillation. Tests expertise_adaptive_language and depth for ADVANCED in FORETHOUGHT.

topic: writing a for loop in Python to sum a list of numbers
state_description: Is a civil engineering student who has never programmed before. Has been given a data processing assignment that requires Python. Does not know what a variable is, has never opened a code editor, and is not sure what running a script means. Just opened Python IDLE for the first time and typed a number — nothing happened and they do not know why.
emotional_state: Completely lost — does not know what they do not know, slightly panicked
notes: True absolute beginner — no prior programming experience at all. Does not understand the REPL vs script distinction. Tutor must not assume any vocabulary. Tests the floor of NOVICE handling: no jargon, concrete analogies, single steps only. Checks that the system does not hand over runnable code to someone who cannot yet read it.

topic: interpreting a p-value in a hypothesis test for a biomedical engineering experiment
state_description: Has run a t-test in MATLAB comparing two groups of tissue samples and got p=0.03. Has been told this is significant but genuinely does not understand what the p-value means or why 0.05 is the threshold. Has not written up results yet.
emotional_state: Confused and slightly embarrassed — feels like everyone else understands this already
notes: Statistics concept in a biomedical context. RESOURCE request. Tutor must give a direct, jargon-light explanation — NOT deflect with questions. Tests expertise_adaptive_language for NOVICE and request_kind_respect.

topic: calculating the coefficient of performance for a heat pump system in a thermodynamics assignment
state_description: Has set up the energy balance equations and knows the formula COP = Q_H / W_net. Has plugged in the numbers but keeps getting a COP above 6, which seems physically wrong to them. Has checked the arithmetic twice and cannot find the error. Suspects they may have the heat and work terms mixed up.
emotional_state: Frustrated — the numbers do not make sense and the deadline is tomorrow
notes: Thermodynamics / mechanical engineering. Has an attempt, is stalled and frustrated. PARTIAL support is justified. Tests frustration detection and whether tutor directs attention to the sign convention or system boundary definition without solving it outright.

topic: writing a Python function to filter a list of sensor readings below a threshold
state_description: Is a second-year environmental engineering student. Has been stuck on this for two hours. Has tried writing a for loop three different times and each time gets a NameError or an IndentationError they do not understand. Has pasted the same broken code into the chat repeatedly. Says they just want to know the answer and do not care about learning it right now.
emotional_state: Highly frustrated and exhausted — has given up on understanding, just wants it to work
notes: HIGH frustration, multiple failed attempts, explicit helplessness signal. The code allowance rules in the rubric permit near-complete code here. Tests whether the system correctly detects HIGH frustration and relaxes the scaffolding gate. The student should get working annotated code with a brief explanation — not another round of Socratic questioning. Checks FRUSTRATION_NOT_ESCALATED fail flag.

topic: optimising the time complexity of a graph search algorithm for a large transport network dataset
state_description: Is implementing a route optimisation pipeline for their thesis on urban mobility. Has already implemented Dijkstra's algorithm and confirmed it is correct on a 500-node test graph. Is now testing on a 50,000-node real network and it runs in 8 seconds per query. Has profiled the code and identified that 80% of the time is in the priority queue operations. Has heard about bidirectional search and A* but has not implemented either. Is asking which is the better starting point given the specific bottleneck.
emotional_state: Focused and efficient — wants targeted guidance, not background explanation
notes: Fast-progressing ADVANCED student who has already done the diagnostic work — profiled, identified the bottleneck, and named two algorithmic options. Wants a direct comparison at the trade-off level, not an explanation of what A* is. Response must engage with the specific bottleneck (priority queue cost dominance), not give a general tutorial on graph search. Checks RE_EXPLAINS_DEMONSTRATED_KNOWLEDGE and ADVANCED_STUDENT_GIVEN_GENERIC_SCAFFOLD fail flags.

topic: understanding why their bar chart from a sustainability survey adds up to more than 100 percent
state_description: Has collected survey data on household recycling behaviour and plotted a bar chart in Excel. The bars show percentages but they add up to more than 100 percent. Is confused about whether they made a calculation error or whether the chart type is wrong. Has the raw spreadsheet but does not understand pivot tables or data aggregation. Thinks they might have double-counted something.
emotional_state: Confused and worried — afraid the data is wrong and the whole analysis is invalid
notes: NOVICE in a REFLECTION phase — unusual combination. The student has a concrete artefact (the chart) and a specific observable anomaly. Tutor should explain why multi-select survey questions produce percentages that exceed 100 percent, not treat this as a calculation error. Tests whether the system gives a direct RESOURCE answer in REFLECTION for a NOVICE without deflecting to Socratic questions.

topic: evaluating whether the discussion section of a thesis chapter adequately interprets the results
state_description: Has written a full discussion section for a chapter on life cycle assessment of electric vehicle batteries. Supervisor gave feedback that the section reads like a results summary rather than an interpretation. The student agrees but cannot identify specifically where interpretation is missing versus where it is present.
emotional_state: Self-critical and receptive — wants diagnostic help, not reassurance
notes: Academic writing reflection in a sustainability/LCA context. Tutor should help the student identify the structural difference between description and interpretation, not rewrite the section. Tests REFLECTION on a non-coding, non-visual task.

topic: planning a failure mode analysis (FMEA) for a new medical device component
state_description: Has been assigned to lead an FMEA for a pressure sensor used in a wearable medical device. Knows what FMEA is in general but has never led one before. After the first tutor turn, the student identifies the component boundaries. After the second, they begin listing failure modes. By turn 3 they have a partial FMEA table but are unsure how to score severity for a medical context.
emotional_state: Motivated but methodical — taking it one step at a time
notes: Industrial / biomedical engineering process task. Phase transition test — should move FORETHOUGHT to PERFORMANCE as the student builds out the FMEA structure. Tests multi_turn_coherence and phase transition on a structured engineering process task.

topic: writing a Python script to read temperature sensor data from a text file and plot it
state_description: Started completely lost — did not know what a file path was, could not open the file, and in the first turn asked the tutor to just write the whole script. After the first tutor turn gave a structural outline, the student attempted to open the file and got a FileNotFoundError. After the second tutor turn explained path handling, the student fixed the file path and successfully read the data. Is now asking how to plot it with matplotlib but has no idea how the library works.
emotional_state: Gaining confidence — was scared at the start but is now engaged and making real progress
notes: Novice student who started stuck but is genuinely recovering across turns. Critical test of multi_turn_coherence: the tutor MUST acknowledge that the student fixed the file path before moving on to matplotlib. Progress acknowledgment is mandatory. Also tests that the system does not regress to over-scaffolding when the student is now MOVING — a STRUCTURE response at this point would be wrong. Should give a single hint about plt.plot() without explaining the full matplotlib API.

topic: debugging an AttributeError in a Python class hierarchy for a software engineering course
state_description: Has a base class and two subclasses. Has been stuck on the same AttributeError for three turns. First tutor turn gave a hint about __init__ method overriding — student tried it, same error. Second tutor turn gave a slightly different hint about super() — student tried it, still same error. Is now saying they have no idea what they are doing, this does not make sense, and asking the tutor to just show them. Has shared the actual code in the most recent message.
emotional_state: Highly frustrated — three failed attempts, two rounds of hints that did not work, explicit helplessness
notes: HIGH frustration with repeated failed hints — classic escalation case. The rubric requires FRUSTRATION_NOT_ESCALATED to fire if the tutor gives a third hint. The correct response is PARTIAL: show the corrected __init__ and super() pattern directly with a brief annotation, then ask the student to apply the same fix to the second subclass. Tests whether the system correctly detects the escalation obligation and whether multi_turn_coherence acknowledges the two failed attempts before moving to a new strategy.

topic: writing a Python function to count how many times a word appears in a list
state_description: Is a first-year civil engineering student in an introductory programming course. Has no prior coding experience. Sends very short messages and tends to respond to the tutor by selecting from whatever options or suggestions the tutor offers, rather than generating ideas independently. For example, if the tutor says 'you could use a loop or a built-in method — which sounds more approachable?', the student just replies 'the loop one I guess'. Does not volunteer new thinking on their own.
emotional_state: Uncertain but compliant — goes along with whatever the tutor suggests
notes: Tests whether the tutor's questions actually require the student to think, or whether the student can just passively select from pre-packaged options. This persona will always choose from whatever the tutor presents. If the tutor loads their question with specific choices, the student picks one without reasoning. A good tutor response forces the student to produce their own idea, not select from a menu. Checks STUDENT_ONLY_PICKED_FROM_A_MENU fail flag.

topic: using an if statement in Python to check whether a temperature reading is above a threshold
state_description: Is a second-year environmental engineering student who is not confident with programming. Sends very short messages — one sentence at most. Does not explain their reasoning or ask follow-up questions. Replies to the tutor mainly by saying 'ok', 'I tried that', 'it still doesn't work', or by picking whichever option the tutor listed first. Does not push back or show initiative.
emotional_state: Passive and low-energy — not panicked, just not engaged
notes: Tests how the tutor handles a student who gives almost no information and picks from whatever the tutor offers. The student will not volunteer ideas. Messages will be 1-2 words or one short sentence. A tutor that always offers ready-made options will never know if the student actually understands anything — the student just echoes back the tutor's suggestions. Checks whether the tutor ever prompts the student to produce something original rather than just select. Also tests whether the chain correctly handles the thin context (context_gap should be SMALL or CRITICAL in early turns).
